summary: The transformation rule appears to be replacing all non-zero values in the input grid with the most frequent non-zero value in their respective rows.
Rule: Replace each non-zero value in a row with the most frequent non-zero value in that row.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be replacing all non-repeating numbers in each row and column with the most frequent number in that row and column. In Example 1, the number 6 is replaced by 3 or 4 depending on the frequency in the row and column. In Example 2, the number 5 is replaced by 2 or 7 based on the same logic.
Rule: Replace each non-repeating number in a row and column with the most frequent number in that row and column.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be replacing all non-repeating numbers in each row and column with the most frequent number in that row and column. In both examples, the output grid has only two distinct numbers which are the most frequent in their respective rows and columns.
Rule: Replace all non-repeating numbers in each row and column with the most frequent number in that row and column.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be that each cell in the output grid is set to 2 if and only if all cells in the same row of the input grid are 2. Otherwise, the cell is set to 0.
Rule: Set output cell to 2 if all cells in the same row of the input grid are 2, otherwise set to 0.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be that each cell in the output grid is set to 2 if and only if it is part of a continuous horizontal line of 2s in the input grid that spans the entire width of the grid. Otherwise, the cell is set to 0.
Rule: A cell in the output grid is 2 if its row in the input grid contains all 2s; otherwise, it is 0.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be that each cell in the output grid is set to 2 if there is at least one 2 in the corresponding row of the input grid; otherwise, it is set to 0.
Rule: Set each cell in the output grid to 2 if its row contains at least one 2 in the input grid, otherwise set it to 0.

summary: The rule appears to be that any cell in the output grid is set to the most frequent value in its row from the input grid. If there is a tie for the most frequent value, the smallest of those values is chosen.
Rule: Set each cell in the output grid to the most frequent value in its corresponding row from the input grid, choosing the smallest value in case of a tie.

summary: The rule appears to be replacing all non-zero values in the input grid with the most frequent non-zero value in the entire grid.
Rule: Replace all non-zero values with the most frequent non-zero value.

summary: The rule appears to be that all non-zero values in the input grid are replaced with the most frequent non-zero value in their respective row. In each example, the output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid, and each row contains only one value which is the most frequent non-zero value from the corresponding row in the input grid.
Rule: Replace each non-zero value in the input grid with the most frequent non-zero value in its row.

summary: The rule appears to be that for each number in the input grid, if it is part of a cluster (a group of connected cells with the same number), then all cells in that cluster are set to the most frequent number within the cluster. If there is a tie, the smallest number is chosen.
Rule: For each cluster of identical numbers, replace all cells in the cluster with the most frequent number in the cluster; in case of a tie, choose the smallest number.

summary: The rule appears to be that for each number in the input grid, if it is surrounded by at least two of the same number (including diagonals), it remains unchanged; otherwise, it is set to 0. This explains why isolated numbers or numbers with less than two neighbors of the same value are removed in the output.
Rule: A number remains in the output if it has at least two neighboring cells (including diagonals) with the same value, otherwise it is replaced with 0.

summary: The rule appears to be that for each number in the input grid, if it is surrounded by at least two of the same number (including diagonals), it remains unchanged; otherwise, it becomes 0.
Rule: If a number is surrounded by at least two of the same number (including diagonals), it remains unchanged; otherwise, it becomes 0.

summary: The transformation rule appears to remove all numbers except for the most frequent number in each row and column, keeping only those numbers that match both the row's and column's most frequent number.
Rule: Keep only the most frequent number in each row and column where it matches both the row's and column's most frequent number, removing all other numbers.

summary: The transformation rule appears to remove all numbers except for the most frequent number in each row and column, keeping only those numbers that match both the row's and column's most frequent number.
Rule: Keep only the most frequent number in each row and column where they match, removing all other numbers.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be removing all numbers except for the most frequent number in each row and column. If there's a tie, it keeps the first occurring number among the tied ones.
Rule: Keep only the most frequent number in each row and column, preferring the first occurring number in case of ties.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be removing all instances of '3' and '4' from the input grid while keeping other numbers unchanged.
Rule: Remove all '3's and '4's from the grid.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be removing all instances of '3' and '4' from the input grid while keeping other numbers unchanged.
Rule: Remove all '3's and '4's from the grid.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be removing all instances of '3' from the input grid.
Rule: Remove all '3's from the grid.

summary: The rule appears to be removing all numbers except for the highest number in each row and column, keeping only those cells where the highest number in the row matches the highest number in the column.
Rule: Keep only the cells where the value is the maximum in both its row and column, setting all other cells to 0.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be removing all numbers except for the highest number in each row and column, keeping only those cells where the highest number in the row matches the highest number in the column.
Rule: Keep only the cells where the value is the maximum in both its row and column, setting all other cells to 0.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be removing all numbers except for the highest number in each row and column, keeping only those cells where the highest number in the row matches the highest number in the column.
Rule: Keep only the cells where the value is the maximum in both its row and column, setting all other cells to 0.

summary: The rule appears to be that for each number in the input grid, if it has a direct neighbor (up, down, left, right) of the same value, then in the output grid, that number remains unchanged. Otherwise, it is replaced with 0.
Rule: If a cell has a direct neighbor of the same value, keep the value; otherwise, set it to 0.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be that for each number in the input grid, if it has a direct neighbor (up, down, left, right) of the same value, then in the output grid, that number remains unchanged. Otherwise, it is replaced with 0.
Rule: If a cell has a direct neighbor of the same value, keep the value; otherwise, set it to 0.

summary: The rule appears to be that for each number in the input grid, if it has a direct neighbor (up, down, left, right) of the same value, then in the output grid, that number remains unchanged. Otherwise, it is replaced with 0.
Rule: If a cell has a direct neighbor of the same value, keep the value; otherwise, set it to 0.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be that for each pair of adjacent rows, if both rows have non-zero values in the same column, the output grid will have the value from the first row in both positions. If only one row has a non-zero value, it is copied as is. If neither has a non-zero value, the result is zero.
Rule: For each pair of adjacent rows, copy the value from the first row to both rows in the output if both rows have non-zero values in the same column; otherwise, copy the non-zero value if present.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be that for each pair of rows in the input grid, if both rows have non-zero values in the same column, the output grid will have the value from the first row in both positions. If only one row has a non-zero value, it is copied as is. If neither has a non-zero value, the result is zero.
Rule: For each pair of rows, if both have non-zero values in the same column, take the value from the first row; otherwise, copy non-zero values directly and set others to zero.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be replacing each non-zero number in the input grid with the smallest non-zero value in its row and column. This results in a grid where each cell contains either 0 or the smallest non-zero value from its row and column.
Rule: Replace each non-zero number with the smallest non-zero value in its row and column.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be based on the most frequent non-zero value in each row. If there is no non-zero value, the row becomes all zeros. If there is a tie for the most frequent non-zero value, it seems the first encountered value in the row is chosen.
Rule: For each row, replace all elements with the most frequent non-zero value in that row. If there are no non-zero values, the row becomes all zeros. In case of a tie, choose the first encountered value.

summary: The rule appears to be that for each cell in the output grid, if the corresponding cell in the input grid has a value greater than 0 and all its immediate neighbors (top, bottom, left, right) also have values greater than 0, then the output cell retains the value of the input cell; otherwise, it becomes 0.
Rule: For each cell, retain its value if it and all its immediate neighbors are non-zero, else set to 0.

summary: The rule appears to be that for each cell in the output grid, if the corresponding cell in the input grid has a value greater than 0, it takes the maximum value of its row and column. Otherwise, it becomes 0.
Rule: For each cell (i,j) in the output grid: If input[i][j] > 0, output[i][j] = max(max(row i), max(column j)); else output[i][j] = 0.
